meta.title: Sand Table on Sand Table — predicted readers + triers of the Pike Substack intro
meta.notes: Mixed cohort: 6 readers + 3 skill-triers, encountering the 5-paragraph Substack guest post + (for triers) attempting first-touch installation. Turn 1-3 = read intro/body/closing; Turn 4 = decide whether to try; Turn 5 = first-touch experience.

agents.name: Maya Singh
agents.role: B2B SaaS PM, validates copy pre-ship

agents.name: Priya Mehra
agents.role: Trier — careful reader, has a real domain in mind

agents.name: Marcus Kane
agents.role: Trier — DevOps, validate streams from existing sim

events.internal_monologue: Military metaphor. I can almost predict where this is going. The author had better get to the point in the next paragraph or I am closing the tab.
Yes, this is a war-game framing. I want to know if the author has read the literature on simulation validity. So far it sounds like rhetoric, not engineering.
I can picture my team around a table with proxy readers. The metaphor lands. I want to know what part of this can replace our copy reviews.
The phrase 'place pieces, argue' is exactly how I run my facilitation training. This is my world.
The line about the green ensign doing the wrong thing at the worst moment — I am stealing that. This author can write.
Wargames have a long, well-documented record of producing confidence that does not survive contact with reality. I will give this five more paragraphs to demonstrate awareness of that.
Scrolling. Looking for a code block.
'Protocol — a small, opinionated standard.' Good. I have been burned by frameworks. A protocol I can adopt incrementally.
Skipping. Where are the inputs and outputs.
Three concrete use cases. That is a useful structure. Reader simulation, training cohort, dev session extraction. The third one is interesting — that is a real telemetry problem.
Cross-domain envelope is the ambitious claim. Are the event schemas actually compatible across these three, or is it a shared shape with three incompatible payloads underneath? The post does not say.
'Where attention drifts, where conviction shifts' — that is the literal language we use in copy reviews. I would pay for this if it works on landing pages.
Six personas through coaching modules. I want to see one. The post is good at the abstract but I need a sample event before I can evaluate.
Section by section is exactly how I want feedback. Right now I get full-essay reactions a week late.
Three faces. Who chose these three? What is being left out? Therapy sessions? Negotiation? The post is not yet defending its own ontology.
Still no commands.
I am noting the abstraction. My domain is annotated PDFs. Does that fit one of the three faces, or is it a fourth?
'Extraction from session logs' — that is exactly the surface I care about. Where is the script.
The independence constraint is the strongest thing in this post. 'One LLM wearing six wigs is not a sand table' — that is a clean line. I am bookmarking.
Five detection signals. I want the false-positive rate on a benchmark. The post claims they exist; the engineering question is calibration. Going to GitHub after this.
'Impossible narrative detection' — the language is getting technical. I am worried this section is for someone other than me.
I have run cohort simulations where one persona predicted what another would say, and I did not have language for why that was wrong. This post gives me the language.
If I run this on my own essay and the personas all sound like me, the data is worthless. Now I know what to look for.
First moment of approval. The author has named the failure mode and shipped a detector. This is what good engineering against your own enthusiasm looks like.
Done. Closing.
This section earned my trust. Going to install.
Less relevant for extraction. Skimming.
'A guide, not a gatekeeper.' I appreciate the modesty. I would actually try this on a small project.
'Variance, deltas, trajectories instead of vibes' is an accurate description of what protocol-driven simulation buys you. Good.
'Does not promise prediction' — wait, then what am I getting? I need a sentence that says specifically what I get for my time.
This matches my facilitation philosophy. A rehearsal makes you honest.
I want a one-liner I can share that captures the essay. The 'honest about what it is' line is close.
The author understands what they do not know. This is the most credible AI-tools post I have read this quarter.
Looking for a link. I do not see a clear next-step button. I would have clicked 'try it on a paragraph of your own writing.'
Going to file an issue requesting the false-positive benchmark on the narrative detector.
Will message a colleague who runs reader research. Not sure if I can use this without her.
Sharing with two colleagues immediately. This is the curriculum-evaluation tool I have been hand-rolling for two years.
Going to try it on my last essay. Wish there were a 'paste your essay here' demo on the post.
Adding to my next paper's reference list.

Ran scaffold.py interactively. It worked. Now it is asking me to choose a temporal model. I do not know what 'bracket' means in this context. The post never used that word. Going to read references/protocol-spec.md.

Validated a stream I had lying around. Got a clamp warning — my scores are 0-100, not 0-5. The default range surprised me. I would have wanted a sentence in the README warning me to set score_range in mappings.json.

events.notes: Opening landed for 5/9; lost 2 readers (theo, marcus) entirely; elena withholding judgment.
Three-use-cases section earned engagement from skeptics (alex, marcus). Elena flagged ontological gap.
Pivotal section. Independence constraint + 5-signal detector earned strongest reactions. Maya disengaged here.
Modesty landed for everyone except Maya, who needed a clearer value statement.
Closing missed an actionable next step. Triers hit two friction points (Priya: bracket terminology not defined; Marcus: score_range default surprise).

summary.weakest_section: turn 4 for Maya specifically; turn 5 for actionability
convergent_findings: The independence constraint is the strongest claim in the post; it earned trust from technical skeptics (Sanjay, Elena) and validated practitioners (Rosa, Jamie).
Maya represents a reader the post may be losing: she wants concrete value statements ('what do I get for my time'), not philosophy.
Theo is unreachable — pre-coffee skim readers will not engage with a metaphor-heavy intro. This may be intentional triage.

Trier friction is concentrated in two specific places: (1) the post never defines 'bracket' temporal model but scaffold.py asks for it, (2) the score_range default of [0,5] surprises users with different scoring conventions.
improvement_recommendations: Add a 'what you get' sentence to paragraph 4 — concrete, not philosophical (e.g., 'a comparable second opinion from six imagined readers in five minutes').
Add an actionable next step to paragraph 5 — a link, a starter command, or a 'paste your essay' demo.
Either define 'bracket' in the post or have scaffold.py print a one-line definition when it asks for the temporal model.
Add a README note on score_range defaults — surface the [0,5] assumption and how to override.
Consider a one-line callout for Sanjay-type readers: link to the false-positive characterization for the 5-signal detector (or document its absence and call it future work).
